coredata.dc:title: Spin polarization tuning in the graphene quantum dot by using in-plane external electric field
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Modarresi, M.
Roknabadi, M.R.
Shahtahmasebi, N.
coredata.dc:description: Electronic, magnetic and transport properties of a nano-graphene dot have been studied by using the DFT and tight binding methods. In the tight binding calculations, the interaction between electrons is modeled using the Hubbard Hamiltonian. By comparison between the eigen-values and density of states in the tight binding and DFT models, we tabulate a set of tight-binding parameters to describe graphene quantum dots for future works. The effects of a single vacancy and an in-plane external electric field on the spin-dependent transport of graphene quantum dot have been investigated. Transport through GQD between two GNR is studied by using Green's function formalism. Our results confirm an intrinsic spin-dependent current and relatively large spin polarization through the GQD in the presence of a single vacancy and zigzag edge. It is also shown that an in-plane external electric field controls the spin-polarization in graphene quantum dot.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Nano-structure
Hubbard model
Spin-polarization
Green's function method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The relaxed structure of (a) GQD and (b) V-GQD.
Density of state in the case of (a) GQD with DFT and tight binding, (b) DFT and (c) tight-binding DOS of spin up/down for V-GQD.
(a) DFT and (b) tight binding calculation of net charge of GQD and net spin of V-GQD in the (c) DFT and (d) tight binding models.
(a) V-GQD and (b) T-GQD between two GNRs as electrodes.
(a) Real and (b) imaginary part of self-energy matrix elements.
(a) The transmission spectrum of GQD in different voltages and (b) spin dependent transmission through V-GQD for minority and majority carriers.
The I–V characteristic and spin-polarization curve of V-GQD between two semi-infinite GNRs.
The variation of SP as a function of applied voltage in the presence of in-plane external electric field.
The value of tight binding parameters.
The value of critical electric field for different T-GQD structures in the unit of V/Å.

head.ce:title: Spin polarization tuning in the graphene quantum dot by using in-plane external electric field

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electronic, magnetic and transport properties of a nano-graphene dot have been studied by using the DFT and tight binding methods. In the tight binding calculations, the interaction between electrons is modeled using the Hubbard Hamiltonian. By comparison between the eigen-values and density of states in the tight binding and DFT models, we tabulate a set of tight-binding parameters to describe graphene quantum dots for future works. The effects of a single vacancy and an in-plane external electric field on the spin-dependent transport of graphene quantum dot have been investigated. Transport through GQD between two GNR is studied by using Green's function formalism. Our results confirm an intrinsic spin-dependent current and relatively large spin polarization through the GQD in the presence of a single vacancy and zigzag edge. It is also shown that an in-plane external electric field controls the spin-polarization in graphene quantum dot.
We study the spin polarization in the presence of an external electric field.

ce:section.ce:para: The discovery of graphene which is an ideal 2-dimensional carbon sheet attracts theoretical and experimental solid-state physicist [1–16]. One-dimensional carbon nano-structures like graphene nano-ribbon (GNR) and carbon nano-tube (CNT) show interesting electronic, optical and magnetic properties in many recent works. Beside these structures the graphene quantum dot (GQD) attracts a lot of theoretical and experimental interest [17–22]. Due to the very long spin relaxation and de-coherence time graphene is an appropriate material for future spintronic devices [2,3,23]. Different tight-binding and density functional calculations suggest an intrinsic magnetic behavior in the graphene associated with the presence of structure defect [2,4–8,24,25]. Just like the GNR in the GQD the zigzag edge breaks the sub-lattice symmetry which creates degenerate state in the Fermi level and leads to finite net macroscopic magnetic moment [11,18,26–30]. Recently Guclu et al. showed the same behavior in the GNR rings in the Mobius configurations [31]. Also, the variation of magnetization as a function of filling factor of the edge states in the zigzag edge of triangle graphene quantum dots [27,30] and GNR rings in the circular and Mobius configurations was reported [31].

The ab-initio methods for nano-structures are computationally expensive but the parametric methods like tight binding are cheap and applicable in the case of large unit-cell and macro-molecules. In the first part of this work we will study the magnetic properties of a single vacancy in the edge of a circular GQD by using the density functional theory (DFT) and tight binding model. By comparison between the results of both models the required tight-binding parameters will be obtained. Here, in the tight-binding calculations the interaction between electrons with different spin is modeled by using the Hubbard model. The Hubbard model was studied in the post Hartree–Fock methods like the configuration interaction (CI) method that takes electron-correlation into account [27,28,30,32]. In this work we neglect the electron-correlation and use the mean-field approximation to solve the Hubbard Hamiltonian [11,26,33,34]. By using the obtained parameters transport through two different kinds of quantum dot between two GNRs is investigated. The Hamiltonian of the GQD is written in the tight binding approximation and the self-energy of left and right GNRs are calculated in the self-consistent manner [9,10]. The spin dependent transmission function is calculated by using the well-known Green's function method [9,10,33,35,36]. An external uniform electric field perturbs the electronic state of GQD and vanishes the degeneracy in the Fermi level [26,28]. In the last part of this work we will study the effect of the external electric-field on the spin-polarization transport in the GQD.

Here, all ab-initio calculations were performed with the SIESTA code using Perdew–Zunger parameterization of local density approximation [37]. The energy cut-off for real space integration is set to 440Ry and Brillouin zone integration is performed with 12×12×1 Monkhorst–Pack grid in the reciprocal space. The free-space between neighbor dots is set to 12Å to avoid interaction between them. In the first part of this study we consider a typical circular graphene quantum dot and investigate the presence of a single vacancy in the edge of dot (V-GQD). Fig. 1 shows the relaxed atomic structure of GQD and V-GQD. In the presence of a single vacancy the nearest neighbor of the removed atom moves towards the vacancy position and a pentagonal structure is formed in the edge of GQD. The bond-length between carbon atoms varies from 1.37Å to 1.45Å and 1.40Å to 1.49Å in the GQD and VGQD, respectively. The new bond between carbon atoms in the DFT calculations makes a new hopping in the tight binding model. The position of next nearest neighbor of vacancy with respect to their adjacent atom is nearly unaffected. In the tight binding calculations the Hamiltonian of GQD is written in the single-band model and interaction between electrons is described in the Hubbard model [12,27,28,32],
(1)
H
G Q D
=
∑
i , σ
ε
i
C
i , σ
†
C
i , σ
+
∑
< i , j > , σ
t
i , j
[
C
i , σ
†
C
j , σ
+
C
j , σ
†
C
i , σ
] + U
∑
i , σ
C
i , σ
†
C
i , σ
C
i , σ '
†
C
i , σ '
where
ε
i
is the on-site energy of carbon and
t
i , j
is the hopping between nearest neighbor atoms.
C
i , σ
†
(
C
i , σ
) is the electron creation (annihilation) operator for an electron with spin σ at site i. The last term in Eq. (1) describes the interaction between electrons with opposite spin on a same site. The strength of interaction which is shown with U will be considered as a parameter in our calculations. In this work the tight binding parameters are obtained by fitting the eigenvalue of GQD in the DFT and tight binding model. A simple Metropolis algorithm is used to reach a better agreement between two models. The Hubbard term is discussed by using the mean-field approach. The expectation value of the number operator is calculated as [12,33],
(2)
〈
n
i , σ
〉 =
− 1
π
∫
− ∞
ε
f
Im [
G
i , i , σ
0
( ε ) ]
d ε

G
σ
0
is the non-interacting Green's function of the quantum dot,
(3)
G
σ
0
( ε ) =
[ ( ε + i
δ
+
) S −
H
G Q D
]
− 1
where S is the overlap matrix between π orbitals of carbon atoms. The diagonal elements of S are 1 and the off-diagonal elements that indicate the overlap between neighbor atoms are obtained by fitting. According to Eq. (1) the Hamiltonian of dot depends on the spin distribution and vice versa. As a result, the interaction term in the Hamiltonian and spin distribution should be solved self-consistently. Due to the difference between the environment of carbon atoms in the center and edge of GQD, we should use different on-site energy for carbon atoms. Fig. 2 shows the DFT and tight binding results for density of state (DOS) in the case of GQD and V-GQD. The HOMO-LUMO gap of GQD in the DFT and tight binding model is about 1.3 and 1.1eV, respectively. Our results indicate spin independent DOS for GQD and the difference between DOS for spin up and down electrons reveals the magnetic properties of V-GQD. The interaction between electrons split the DOS of spin up and down which is important around the Fermi level and tends to zero in higher energy. The splitting between the DOS of spin up and down which is proportional to the magnitude of the Hubbard term is about 0.3eV around the Fermi level in the DFT and tight binding model. The resulted parameters for tight binding model which include the different on-site energy, hopping, overlap and Hubbard term are collected in Table 1. Where the
ε
c
and
ε
s
are the on-site energy of carbon atoms at the center and edge of quantum dot. The hopping integral between nearest carbon atoms t
C–C has a good agreement with previous works [9,13]. The reported value of U/t is somehow different from 0.9 [38] to 1.3 [11]. The magnitude of U/t is limited to 2.33 above which the Mott–Hubbard transition occurred [11]. The local net charge of carbon sites of GQD in the DFT and tight binding models is plotted in Fig. 3a and b, respectively. Fig. 3c and d shows the DFT and tight-binding results of the net spin of carbon atoms in the V-GQD. The net spin is defined as the difference between the densities of electrons with opposite spin direction on each site. For more convenience in the comparison between the DFT and tight binding results the un-relaxed geometry of V-GQD is used to plot the net spin of carbon atoms. Both models show spin neutrality of atoms in the center of V-GQD and the localization of majority spin on the zigzag edge of the dot. According to our DFT results the main contribution to the net spin comes from the Pz orbitals of carbon atoms that validate the single orbital tight binding model. Although DFT calculation for bigger quantum dot is expensive but the generalization of tight-binding model for other structures is a relatively simple task. In the last part of this work we will study transport through GQD, V-GQD and triangular graphene quantum dot (T-GQD) between two GNR as shown in Fig. 4. Also, the presence of an external electric field on the spin-polarized current through the T-GQD is investigated. In the T-GQD, variation of N 1 and N 2 will control the number of edge state and magnetization. The total Hamiltonian of system includes the Hamiltonian of left and right nano-ribbons
H
G N R , ( L / R )
, quantum dot
H
G Q D
and the coupling between them
H
T
.
(4)
H
t o t
=
H
G Q D
+
H
G N R , L
+
H
G N R , R
+
H
T
The interaction between electrons in the left/right GNR is neglected in this work. The presence of nano-ribbons on both side of the dot considered as a perturbation on the Hamiltonian of GQD. Green's function of the GNR/GQD/GNR is calculated by using the Dyson relation,
(5)
G
σ
( ε ) =
G

) S −

]
− 1
where
Σ
G N R
r
=
Σ
G N R , L
r
+
Σ
G N R , R
r
is the retarded self-energy of left/right electrodes which describes the presence of GNR on both sides of GQD. The self-energy for both electrodes is calculated as
Σ
G N R ( L / R )
=
H
G N R , G Q D
+
g
G N R
H
G N R , G Q D
[9,10,39]. Where
H
G N R , G Q D
shows the coupling between the GQD and the GNRs on both side of dot. The
g
G N R
is the surface Green's function of the semi-infinite GNR. Adding a new layer to the semi-infinite lead does not change the surface Green's function as a result the surface Green's function can be calculated in the self-consistent manner [9]. According to the mean-field Hubbard model the on-site energy for minority and majority electrons is changed. The spin dependent transmission through the GNR/GQD/GNR can be calculated as [12,18,35,36,39,40],
(6)
T r ( ε ) = Trace [
Γ
G N R ( L )
( ε − e
φ
L
)
G
σ
( ε )
Γ
G N R ( R )
( ε + e
φ
R
)
G
σ †
( ε ) ]
where
φ
L / R
is the electrical potential of left/right electrode, T r ( ε ) is the transmission as a function of incident energy and
Γ
G N R ( L / R )
= − 2 Im [
Σ
G N R ( L / R )
] . The coupling strength between the GNR and GQD is considered the half of hopping between carbon atoms in the dot. The self-energy of both electrodes has only one non-zero element which is plotted as a function of energy in Fig. 5. The real and imaginary parts of self-energy describe the energy shift and the broadening of different energy levels, respectively. In the limit of ε ⪡
t
C – C
the wideband approximation that neglect the real part of self-energy and approximate the imaginary part with a constant value is quite an acceptable approach [41,42]. Fig. 6a and b represents the transmission spectrum as a function of energy through GQD in different voltages and spin-dependent transport in the V-GQD in zero bias voltage. The transmission peaks are relatively voltage independent and correspond to the eigenvalues of nano-structure. In the case of V-GQD the transmission becomes spin dependent due to the presence of net spin on the carbon sites. The spin polarization does not affect the electronic energy levels in higher energies and the main effect is limited around the Fermi level. It should be noted that the spin-dependent transport is not related to any external magnetic field or magnetic electrodes, it is an intrinsic property of graphene quantum dot. The current is calculated by using the Landuer–Butiker formula [9,10,33]. By increasing the applied voltage on the both sides of the GQD the energy window in the central region opens and new states contribute in the current. Fig. 7 shows the current–voltage characteristic for electrons with spin up and down. In the low bias voltage spin up and down currents tend to zero and in some certain voltage regions I–V curve has steps due to the transmission peaks. Current in the V-GQD is intrinsically spin polarized, the spin-polarization coefficient which is defined as, S P =
I
↑
−
I
↓
/
I
↑
+
I
↓
is plotted in Fig. 7. In the low voltage region the SP is large but current is not enough to detect. By increasing applied voltage the I–V curve of different spins splits but the SP tends to zero. The variation of SP and current–voltage characteristic as a function of voltage lead us to determine an appropriate work-voltage for future spintronic based devices. In the following part of the article we will study the effect of an external electric field on the spin-polarized transport through T-GQD as shown in Fig. 4b. For the sake of simplicity the self-energy of electrodes are described in the wide-band approximation. The imaginary part of self-energy is set to −0.35eV for all range of energies. In the presence of an external electric field with strength E, the on-site energy of each atomic position shifts according to
ε
i
→
ε
i
− e E
Y
i
. The net magnetic moment in the T-GQD comes from the degenerate edge states that are localized at the edges of dot. In the absence of the electric field the quasi-degenerate states in the vicinity of Fermi energy cause a relatively large net spin in the triangular dots [26–28,30,32]. By applying an external electric field, the one-site energy of edge atoms will be changed. As a result, the degenerate states that come from the edge atoms will be disappeared in the presence of the external field and the net moment decreases [26]. Fig. 8 shows the spin polarization ratio of T-GQD in the presence of external field. The maximum value of SP is 1 which shows the pure spin up current. The negative SP shows that the dominant current in a certain voltage interval comes from the electron with spin down. In the presence of the external electric field the net magnetic moment and SP decrease. In general by increasing the bias voltage and external electric field SP decreases. In the case of T-GQD with even number of atoms, the net spin and SP is zero above a critical electric field which is tabulated for selected structures in Table 2. The zero value of SP is equivalent to the spin-independent transport. By increasing the wide of graphene dot the voltage difference on both side of dot increases and the critical electric field decreases. Our results indicate a macroscopic magnetic behavior and show the tuning of spin-polarized current in a typical graphene quantum dot by using an in-plane external electric field.

In summary, we have studied the electronic, magnetic and transport properties of V-GQD and T-GQD. The interaction between electrons in the dot region is modeled by using the mean field Hubbard Hamiltonian. The DFT and tight binding models show a net magnetization in the presence of single vacancy in the graphene dot. Our results confirm the localization of spin on the zigzag edges of dot and spin dependent transport through GNR/GQD/GNR structure. A relatively large spin polarization is shown in the special form of graphene quantum dot. Also we study the tuning of spin-polarization through quantum dot in the presence of an in-plane external electric field.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Michael Wimmer, Quantum transport in nanostructures: From computational concepts to spintronics in graphene and magnetic tunnel junctions. Dissertationsreihe der Fakultät für Physik der Universität Regensburg 5, PhD. Universität Regensburg, 2009.

Energy Transfer Dynamics in Biomaterial Systems
